chapter_info.subject: Science
chapter_info.chapter_name: The Invisible Living World: Beyond Our Naked Eye

flashcards.a: A cell is the basic unit of life; the smallest structure capable of performing all life processes independently. All living organisms are composed of one or more cells.

A cell membrane is the thin outer boundary of a cell that controls the entry of essential materials and exit of waste products. It separates the cell from its surroundings and maintains cell integrity.

Cytoplasm is a gel-like substance filling the space between the cell membrane and nucleus. It contains cellular components and compounds such as carbohydrates, proteins, fats, and mineral salts. Most life processes occur in cytoplasm.
The nucleus is a membrane-bound structure in eukaryotic cells containing genetic material. It regulates all cellular activities and controls cell growth and reproduction.
A cell wall is a rigid outer layer surrounding the cell membrane in plant, fungal, and bacterial cells. It provides structural support and strength to the cell.
A vacuole is a large, empty-looking space in plant cells that stores important substances, helps remove waste products, and maintains cell shape and rigidity.
A chloroplast is an organelle in plant cells containing chlorophyll, the green pigment. Chloroplasts enable photosynthesis, the process by which plants produce their own food using sunlight.
A nucleoid is the region in bacterial cells containing genetic material but lacking a nuclear membrane. This distinguishes prokaryotic bacteria from eukaryotic cells.
A tissue is a group of similar cells working together to perform a specific function in an organism.
An organ is a structure composed of different tissues organized together to perform a complex function.
An organ system is a group of organs working together to perform a major body function.
An organism is a complete living being composed of all organ systems functioning together.

flashcards.q: What is the organizational hierarchy of life?
flashcards.a: Cell → Tissue (group of similar cells) → Organ (different tissues) → Organ System (organs working together) → Organism (complete living being).
A multicellular organism is a living being composed of many specialized cells that cooperate to perform complex functions. Examples include humans, plants, and animals.
A unicellular organism is a living being composed of a single cell that independently performs all life functions. Examples include bacteria and some protozoa like Amoeba.
A microorganism is a microscopic living creature too small to be seen with the naked eye. Requires magnification of 100-400 times to observe. Also called a microbe.
Bacteria are single-celled prokaryotic microorganisms lacking a true nucleus. They have a nucleoid region instead. Come in various shapes: spherical, rod, spiral, and comma-shaped.
Protozoa are single-celled eukaryotic microorganisms. Examples include Amoeba and Paramecium. Found in water and soil; move using specialized structures.
A fungus is a eukaryotic organism that obtains nutrition by decomposing organic matter. Examples: yeast, mould, mushrooms. Can be unicellular or multicellular; lacks chlorophyll.
Algae are plant-like microorganisms containing chlorophyll. Produce their own food through photosynthesis. Can be single or multicellular; found in water and soil.
Yeast is a single-celled fungus that carries out fermentation. Breaks down sugars to produce carbon dioxide gas and small amounts of alcohol. Used in bread-making and fermentation.
Rhizobium is a nitrogen-fixing bacterium that lives symbiotically in root nodules of legume plants. Converts atmospheric nitrogen into usable nitrogen compounds for plant growth.
Lactobacillus is a bacterium that ferments milk to produce curd. Produces lactic acid as a fermentation byproduct, giving curd its sour taste.
A microalga is a microscopic plant-like organism containing chlorophyll. Produces more than half of Earth's oxygen through photosynthesis. Examples: Spirulina, Chlorella, Diatoms.
A virus is a microscopic, acellular infectious agent. Can only reproduce inside living host cells. May infect plants, animals, or bacteria; often causes disease.
Fermentation is a metabolic process where microorganisms break down organic compounds (usually sugars) to produce energy. Produces gases (like CO₂) or acids (like lactic acid) as byproducts.
Decomposition is the process where microorganisms break down dead organic matter (plant and animal waste) into simpler substances, recycling nutrients back to soil.
Compost is dark-colored, nutrient-rich material formed by microbial decomposition of organic waste. Used as natural fertilizer to enrich soil for plant growth.
Nitrogen fixation is the process where bacteria convert atmospheric nitrogen (N₂) into usable nitrogen compounds (nitrates) that plants can absorb and utilize for growth.
Symbiosis is a close relationship between two organisms where both benefit from the association. Example: Rhizobium bacteria in legume root nodules provide nitrogen; plants provide carbohydrates.
A microscope is an optical instrument that magnifies small objects 100-400 times, enabling observation of microorganisms and cellular structures invisible to the naked eye.
A foldscope is a low-cost, portable, foldable paper microscope providing accessible microscopic observation. Makes microbiology education accessible without expensive laboratory equipment.
A prokaryote is a cell or organism lacking a membrane-bound nucleus. Bacteria are prokaryotes; they have a nucleoid region instead of a true nucleus.
A eukaryote is a cell or organism with a membrane-bound nucleus. Includes plant cells, animal cells, fungal cells, and protist cells.

Robert Hooke observed cork tissue under his microscope and identified tiny compartments that resembled a honeycomb. He named these compartments 'cells,' introducing the term 'cell' to science for the first time.

flashcards.q: Who is known as the Father of Microbiology?
flashcards.a: Antonie van Leeuwenhoek is known as the Father of Microbiology. He developed better lenses and microscopes and was the first to clearly observe and describe tiny living things like bacteria and blood cells.

A root nodule is a swollen structure on plant roots that contains nitrogen-fixing bacteria like Rhizobium. The bacteria live symbiotically, converting atmospheric nitrogen into forms usable by the plant.

Biogas is a mixture of gases (primarily methane and carbon dioxide) produced when anaerobic bacteria decompose plant and animal waste or wastewater. Used as a fuel source for cooking, heating, and electricity.

flashcards.q: How do anaerobic bacteria differ from aerobic bacteria?
flashcards.a: Anaerobic bacteria live in oxygen-free environments and do not require oxygen for respiration. Aerobic bacteria require oxygen for respiration. Anaerobic bacteria are used in biogas production.

flashcards.q: Why is safranin stain used in microscopy?
flashcards.a: Safranin is a red-colored stain used to color cells and make them visible under the microscope. It provides contrast, making transparent cellular structures distinguishable and easier to observe.

flashcards.q: Why is glycerin used when mounting cells on microscope slides?
flashcards.a: Glycerin is used to prevent cells from drying out during microscopic observation. It maintains cell moisture and improves the clarity and visibility of cellular structures.

flashcards.q: What is a meniscus in relation to microscope preparation?
flashcards.a: In microscope preparation, a meniscus is the curved surface of liquid under a coverslip. Proper placement of the coverslip creates an even meniscus, ensuring the slide is well-prepared for observation.

flashcards.q: Why are microorganisms found everywhere on Earth?
flashcards.a: Microorganisms are found everywhere because they are highly adaptable, can survive in diverse environments (water, soil, air, extreme temperatures), and are extremely small, making them easy to disperse and accumulate in various locations.

flashcards.q: Why do some foods like pickles and murabbas resist microbial spoilage?
flashcards.a: Pickles and murabbas contain high concentrations of salt or sugar as preservatives. These high osmotic concentrations prevent microorganism growth by creating unfavorable conditions; microbes cannot survive or reproduce in such environments.

flashcards.q: Why is temperature important for yeast fermentation?
flashcards.a: Yeast ferments most efficiently in warm conditions. Temperature affects enzyme activity within yeast cells. Warm temperatures (around 40°C) accelerate yeast respiration and gas production; cold temperatures slow these processes dramatically.

flashcards.q: Why is refrigeration effective for food preservation?
flashcards.a: Refrigeration slows bacterial growth and metabolism dramatically by reducing temperature. At cold temperatures, microorganisms grow slowly and ferment slowly, extending the shelf life of perishable foods.

flashcards.q: Why do legume farmers plant crops in rotation with nitrogen-fixing bacteria present?
flashcards.a: Legumes (peas, beans) have nitrogen-fixing bacteria in their roots that enrich soil with usable nitrogen. Planting legumes naturally increases soil nitrogen, reducing the need for chemical fertilizers in subsequent crops.

flashcards.q: Why are microalgae important for oxygen balance on Earth?
flashcards.a: Microalgae produce more than half of Earth's atmospheric oxygen through photosynthesis. They are primary producers in aquatic ecosystems and maintain oxygen levels essential for all aerobic life.

flashcards.q: Fill in: The cell _____ controls the entry and exit of materials.
flashcards.a: membrane

flashcards.q: Fill in: Bacteria lack a true _____ and instead have a nucleoid.
flashcards.a: nucleus

flashcards.q: Fill in: A _____ is a group of similar cells working together.
flashcards.a: tissue

flashcards.q: Fill in: _____ organisms are composed of a single cell.
flashcards.a: Unicellular

flashcards.q: Fill in: _____ organisms are composed of many cells.
flashcards.a: Multicellular

flashcards.q: Fill in: _____ is a process where microorganisms break down dead organic matter.
flashcards.a: Decomposition

flashcards.q: Fill in: _____ is a metabolic process where microorganisms produce gases or acids from sugars.
flashcards.a: Fermentation

flashcards.q: Fill in: _____ bacteria live symbiotically in legume root nodules and fix nitrogen.
flashcards.a: Rhizobium

flashcards.q: Fill in: _____ is a bacterium that ferments milk to produce curd.
flashcards.a: Lactobacillus

flashcards.q: Fill in: Plant cells contain a large _____ for storage and structural support.
flashcards.a: vacuole

flashcards.q: True or False: All cells contain a nucleus with a nuclear membrane.
flashcards.a: False. Bacterial cells (prokaryotes) lack a true nucleus; they have a nucleoid instead.

flashcards.q: True or False: Plant cells have a cell wall; animal cells do not.
flashcards.a: True. The cell wall in plant cells provides rigidity and strength; animal cells have only a flexible cell membrane.

flashcards.q: True or False: Microorganisms are found only in water and soil.
flashcards.a: False. Microorganisms are found everywhere—in water, soil, air, food, and inside living organisms including the human body.

flashcards.q: True or False: Yeast produces carbon dioxide gas during fermentation.
flashcards.a: True. Yeast ferments sugars and produces CO₂ gas, which creates bubbles that make bread rise and become fluffy.

flashcards.q: True or False: Lactobacillus bacteria require cold temperatures to ferment milk into curd.
flashcards.a: False. Lactobacillus ferments milk most efficiently in warm conditions. Cold temperatures slow or prevent curd formation.

flashcards.q: True or False: Fungi are producers that make their own food through photosynthesis.
flashcards.a: False. Fungi are decomposers; they lack chlorophyll and cannot photosynthesize. They obtain nutrition by breaking down organic matter.

flashcards.q: True or False: Algae produce more than half of Earth's atmospheric oxygen.
flashcards.a: True. Microalgae produce more than half of Earth's oxygen through photosynthesis in oceans and freshwater.

flashcards.q: True or False: Nitrogen-fixing bacteria harm legume plants by living in their roots.
flashcards.a: False. Nitrogen-fixing bacteria like Rhizobium have a symbiotic relationship with legumes—both organisms benefit. Bacteria provide nitrogen; plants provide carbohydrates.

flashcards.q: True or False: The nucleus is called the control center because it regulates all cellular activities.
flashcards.a: True. The nucleus contains genetic material that directs all cell functions, growth, and reproduction.

flashcards.q: True or False: Viruses are cells with genetic material.
flashcards.a: False. Viruses are acellular—they lack cellular structure. They are infectious particles that reproduce only inside host cells.
